label: Formula 7.14a
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Probability of Fixation Under Additive Selection
nearby_text: For effectively neutral mutations destined to loss, $ |S| < 1.0 $, $$ \Phi_{l}(x)\simeq\frac{N_{e}(1-x)}{N\lambda x} $$ (7.14a) where $ \lambda = 1 - [1/(2N)] $, whereas the unconditional residence time is $$ \Phi(x)\simeq\frac{N_{e}}{Nx} $$ (7.14b) i.e., the average time spent in frequency class x is inversely proportional t

label: Formula 7.14b
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Probability of Fixation Under Additive Selection
nearby_text: fectively neutral mutations destined to loss, $ |S| < 1.0 $, $$ \Phi_{l}(x)\simeq\frac{N_{e}(1-x)}{N\lambda x} $$ (7.14a) where $ \lambda = 1 - [1/(2N)] $, whereas the unconditional residence time is $$ \Phi(x)\simeq\frac{N_{e}}{Nx} $$ (7.14b) i.e., the average time spent in frequency class x is inversely proportional to x.

label: Formula 7.16
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Probability of Fixation Under Additive Selection
nearby_text: (derived in Example A1.8). The mean number of generations until the complete loss of a new mutation with a deleterious heterozygous effect of s < 0 is $$ t_{l}\simeq2(N_{e}/N)[\ln(2N/|S|)+0.423] $$ (7.16) provided $ |S| \gg 1 $ (Kimura and Ohta 1969b; Nei 1971). More general expressions, which require some numerical integration, can be found in Kimura and Ohta (1969a).

  "nearby_text": "(derived in Example A1.8). The mean number of generations until the complete loss of a new mutation with a deleterious heterozygous effect of s < 0 is $$ t_{l}\\simeq2(N_{e}/N)[\\ln(2N/|S|)+0.423] $$ (7.16) provided $ |S| \\gg 1 $ (Kimura and Ohta 1969b; Nei 1971). More general expressions, which require some numerical integration, can be found in Kimura and Ohta (1969a)."
}

label: Formula 7.17a
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Probability of Fixation Under Additive Selection
nearby_text: es descendent from a mutation prior to its loss or fixation is useful in a number of contexts, e.g., determining the total number of individuals affected by a deleterious mutation. This is defined as $$ \overline{n}=\sum_{y=1}^{2N-1}\Phi(y/2N)\cdot y $$ (7.17a) with a shift of the function $ \Phi $ to $ \Phi_{l} $ or $ \Phi_{f} $, leading to the expected numbers conditional on loss or fixation, respectively. label: Formula 7.18a
section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Probability of Fixation Under Arbitrary Selection
nearby_text: dominance, with the genotypes aa, Aa, and AA having fitnesses of 1, 1 + sh, and 1 + 2s, respectively. Diffusion theory (as developed in Appendix 1) then shows the fixation probability of allele A as $$ u_{f}(p_{0}\mid s,h)\simeq\frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{p_{0}}e^{G(x)}\; dx}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}e^{G(x)}\; dx} $$ (7.18a) where $$ G(x)=-4N_{e}s x(h-x) $$ (7.18b)

nearby_text: For a new mutant introduced as a single copy, $ p_0 = 1/(2N) $, under random mating and at least partial dominance, $$ \begin{align*}u_f\left({1\over2N}\right)\simeq{2N_e sh\over N[1-e^{-4N_e sh}]}\end{align*} $$ (7.19a)

label: Formula 7.19b
section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Probability of Fixation Under Arbitrary Selection
nearby_text: terms in the approximation of Equation 7.18a are required. However, for strong positive selection on homozygotes of a completely recessive allele $ (4N_e s \gg 1) $, a close approximation is given by $$ u_{f}\left(\frac{1}{2N}\right)\simeq\frac{\sqrt{4N_{e}s/\pi}}{N} $$ (7.19b)

label: Formula 7.20a
section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Probability of Fixation Under Arbitrary Selection
nearby_text: If there is direct inbreeding due to the mating of close relatives (beyond the amount of long-term inbreeding that is naturally generated by drift), Equation 7.18a will still hold, but now with $$ G(x)=-4N_{e}s x[2f+(1-f)(h-x)] $$ (7.20a) where f is a measure of the departure of genotypes from Hardy-Weinberg expectations, defined (in Chapter 2) by the frequency of heterozygotes, $ 2p(1-p)(1-f) $ (Caballero and Hill 1992b). label: Formula 7.25
section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Fixation of Overdominant and Underdominant Alleles
nearby_text: $, such that after passage through a fitness bottleneck, fixation of the underdominant allele leads to an increase in mean population fitness. section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Fixation of Overdominant and Underdominant Alleles
nearby_text: llele effectively behaves like a deleterious mutation being removed from the population at rate s, and it can be shown that there is essentially no chance of the allele drifting to $ \widehat{p} $ if $$ N_{e}^{*}>\frac{t+2s}{s^{2}} $$ (7.26)

  "nearby_text": "or compared to those induced by drift, we can use Equation 5.1b to show that the expected per-generation frequency change for an allele in the jth generation of additive selection can be described as $$ \\begin{align*}E(\\Delta p_j)\\simeq sE[p_j(1-p_j)]\\simeq sp_0(1-p_0)\\left(1-{1\\over2N_e}\\right)^j\\end{align*} $$ (7.27) where $ p_{0} $ is the initial allele frequency. The last approximation follows directly from the expression for the expected heterozygosity for a neutral locus in a finite population after j generat"
}

label: Formula 7.28a
section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Expected Allele Frequency in a Particular Generation
nearby_text: population after j generations with a starting allele frequency of $ p_{0} $ (Equation 2.5). Summing over generations reveals that the expected frequency after t generations of selection and drift is $$ \begin{align*}E(p_t)&=p_0+\sum_{j=0}^TE(\Delta p_j)\simeq p_0+sp_0(1-p_0)\sum_{j=0}^t\left(1-\frac{1}{2N_e}\right)^j\\&\simeq p_0+2N_e s p_0(1-p_0)\left(1-e^{-t/2N_e}\right)\end{align*} $$ (7.28a) where the last step follows from the useful approximation for large values of $ N_{e} $ $$ \sum_{j=0}^{t}\left(1-\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\right)^{j}\simeq2N_{e}\left(1-e^{-t/2N_{e}}\right) $$ (7.28b)

Summing over generations reveals that the expected frequency after t generations of selection and drift is $$ \\begin{align*}E(p_t)&=p_0+\\sum_{j=0}^TE(\\Delta p_j)\\simeq p_0+sp_0(1-p_0)\\sum_{j=0}^t\\left(1-\\frac{1}{2N_e}\\right)^j\\\\&\\simeq p_0+2N_e s p_0(1-p_0)\\left(1-e^{-t/2N_e}\\right)\\end{align*} $$ (7.28a) where the last step follows from the useful approximation for large values of $ N_{e} $ $$ \\sum_{j=0}^{t}\\left(1-\\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\\right)^{j}\\simeq2N_{e}\\left(1-e^{-t/2N_{e}}\\right) $$ (7.28b)"
}

label: Formula 7.29
section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Expected Allele Frequency in a Particular Generation
nearby_text: More generally, if the genotypes aa, Aa, and AA have fitnesses of 1, 1 + hs, and 1 + 2s, respectively, then for small values of $ N_e|s| $ and $ N_e|sh| $, the expected frequency of A is $$ E(p_{t})\simeq p_{0}+2N_{e}sp_{0}(1-p_{0})\bigg[\left(1-e^{-t/2N_{e}}\right)+\frac{(h-1)(1-2p_{0})}{3}\left(1-e^{-3t/2N_{e}}\right)\bigg] $$ (7.29)

label: Formula 7.30
section: SELECTION AND DRIFT AT SINGLE LOCI
subsection: Expected Allele Frequency in a Particular Generation
nearby_text: Thus, taking the limit of Equation 7.29 as $ t \to \infty $ yields a useful expression for the probability of fixation under weak selection and arbitrary dominance $$ u_{f}(p_{0})\simeq p_{0}+2N_{e}sp_{0}(1-p_{0})\left(1+\frac{(h-1)(1-2p_{0})}{3}\right) $$ (7.30)

  "nearby_text": "Thus, taking the limit of Equation 7.29 as $ t \\to \\infty $ yields a useful expression for the probability of fixation under weak selection and arbitrary dominance $$ u_{f}(p_{0})\\simeq p_{0}+2N_{e}sp_{0}(1-p_{0})\\left(1+\\frac{(h-1)(1-2p_{0})}{3}\\right) $$ (7.30)"

section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: JOINT INTERACTION OF SELECTION, DRIFT, AND MUTATION
nearby_text: ated with different gametic states. Note that when both mutation rates are substantially $<1/(4N_e)$, conditions that may frequently be met for single nucleotide sites (Chapter 4), this simplifies to $$ \phi(x)\simeq\frac{C\overline{W}^{2N_{e}}}{x(1-x)} $$ (7.31b) showing that with weak mutation pressure, the expected allele frequencies that are conditioned on the population being polymorphic are independent of both the mutation rate and the mutation bias.

nearby_text: N_e} \simeq e^{-2N_e y} $ for small values of $ y $, so that $ \overline{W}^{2N_e} \simeq e^{-2N_e s x^2} $, and ignoring back mutation to the advantageous allele, yields the equilibrium distribution $$ \phi(x)=C e^{-2N_{e}s x^{2}}x^{4N_{e}\mu-1}\left(1-x\right)^{-1}\quad for0<x<1 $$ (7.34) a result originally due to Wright (1938b).

label: Formula 7.35
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: JOINT INTERACTION OF SELECTION, DRIFT, AND MUTATION
nearby_text: endent of population size, and provided that $ 2N_{e}hs \gg 1 $ (i.e., the power of selection against heterozygotes exceeds the power of drift), the variance in allele-frequency will be approximately $$ \sigma^{2}(p)=\widetilde{p}/(4N_{e}h s) $$ (7.35)

label: Formula 7.37
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: JOINT INTERACTION OF SELECTION, DRIFT, AND MUTATION
nearby_text: to the optimal state be v and the total mutation rate of the optimal nucleotide to the other states be $ \mu $, it follows from Equation 7.32 that the expected frequency of the optimal nucleotide is $$ \begin{align*}\widetilde{P}_{\rm opt}\simeq{(v/\mu)e^S\over1+(v/\mu)e^S}\end{align*} $$ (7.37)

label: Formula 7.38
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: JOINT INTERACTION OF SELECTION, DRIFT, AND MUTATION
nearby_text: metric mutation ( $ \mu = v $). If we let $ S_i = 4N_e s_i $ (assuming diploidy), the equilibrium probability that i is the fixed (or nearly so) allele is completely independent of the mutation rate, $$ \widetilde{p}_{i}=\frac{e^{S_{i}}}{T},\quad\mathrm{w h e r e}\quad T=\sum_{i=1}^{n}e^{S_{i}} $$ (7.38) and $n$ is the number of alleles.   "nearby_text": "metric mutation ( $ \\mu = v $). Whereas the Sella-Hirsh model makes no assumptions about fitness ordering between alleles, Lynch's (2012b) model assumes there will be an ordered fitness increase in"

label: Formula 7.39
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: JOINT INTERACTION OF SELECTION, DRIFT, AND MUTATION
nearby_text: e constant $k$ setting the granularity of fitness change between adjacent alleles, and a fitness of 1.0 being approached asymptotically as $i \to \infty$. In this case, the stationary distribution is $$ \widetilde{p}_{i}=\frac{(v/\mu)^{i}e^{-S_{i}}}{T},\quad\mathrm{w h e r e}\quad T=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}(v/\mu)^{i}e^{-S_{i}} $$ (7.39) and $ S_i = 4N_e e^{-ki} $.

section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: HALDANE'S PRINCIPLE AND THE MUTATION LOAD
nearby_text: o complete dominance and with heterozygote a fitness of 1 - hs. If we recall from Equation 7.6d that the equilibrium allele frequency is $ \widetilde{p} = \mu / (hs) $, the mean population fitness is $$ \begin{align*}\overline{W}&=1-2hs\widetilde{p}(1-\widetilde{p})-s\widetilde{p}^2\\&\simeq1-2hs\widetilde{p}=1-2hs\left(\frac{\mu}{hs}\right)=1-2\mu\end{align*} $$ (7.40b)

label: Formula 7.42a
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: HALDANE'S PRINCIPLE AND THE MUTATION LOAD
nearby_text: 37. Noting that the load associated with a fixed deleterious mutation is the homozygous effect, 2s, multiplied by the expected fraction of time for which the deleterious allele is fixed, we then have $$ \begin{aligned}L&=2s(1-\widetilde{P}_{opt})=\frac{2s\mu/v}{e^{S}+(\mu/v)}\\&\simeq\frac{2s\mu/v}{1+4N_{e}s+(\mu/v)}\end{aligned} $$ (7.42a)

label: Formula 7.43
section: FIXATION ISSUES INVOLVING TWO LOCI
subsection: The Hill-Robertson Effect
nearby_text: 2). Assuming completely additive selection (no dominance or epistasis), with each copy of A adding $ s_1 $ and each copy of B adding $ s_2 $ to total fitness, the probability that A becomes fixed is $$ u_{f}(p_{0})\simeq p_{0}+2N_{e}s_{1}p_{0}(1-p_{0})+\frac{2N_{e}s_{2}}{2N_{e}c+1}D_{0} $$ (7.43) provided that $ 2N_e|s_1| $ and $ 2N_e|s_2| < 1 $.   "nearby_text": "$ (as most resident gametes are of the type AB, with equivalent fitness to ab as noted above). Thus, the rate of establishment of the ab type by stochastic tunneling to an equivalent fitness state is $$ r_{e}\\simeq(2\\mu/s)(\\mu)=\\frac{2\\mu^{2}}{s} $$ (7.48)"

label: Formula 7.49
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Stochastic Tunneling
nearby_text: els by selection-mutation balance, and assuming equal back and forward mutation rates, Higgs (1998) elegantly showed that the stationary distribution for the frequency $ (x_{0}) $ of the AB gamete is $$ \phi(x_{0})=\frac{1}{(1-z)^{2\alpha-1}}\frac{\Gamma(2\alpha)}{\Gamma(\alpha)^{2}}\left[x_{0}(1-z-x_{0})\right]^{\alpha-1} $$ (7.49) where $ \alpha = 8N\mu^2/s $ is the population rate of mutational production of ab gametes, $ z = 2\mu/s $ is the summed frequency of the Ab and aB gametes, and $ \Gamma $ denotes the gamma function

nearby_text: a first-step mutation. For the stochastic-tunneling domain, however, Equation 7.48 must be modified to account for the fact that the fixation probability of the double mutant is $ \sim 2s_b(N_e/N) $. $$ r_{e}\simeq(2\mu/s_{d})(2N\mu)[2s_{b}(N_{e}/N)]=\frac{8N_{e}\mu^{2}s_{b}}{s_{d}} $$ (7.50)

nearby_text: Komarova et al. 2003; Iwasa et al. 2004; Weissman et al. 2009, 2010; Lynch and Abegg 2010). With $ 4N\mu $ first-step mutations arising per generation, the rate of establishment via tunneling is then $$ r_{e}\simeq4N\mu\sqrt{2\mu s N_{e}/N}=4\mu\sqrt{2\mu s N_{e}N} $$ (7.52)

label: Formula 7.53b
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Stochastic Tunneling
nearby_text: with the final stage being initiated at the one remaining site and involving tunneling within the sublineage containing the first two mutations. For an arbitrary $ d $, this expression generalizes to $$ r_{e}=d\lambda\mu(2N\mu)^{1-0.5^{d-1}}S^{0.5^{d-1}} $$ (7.53b) where $ S = 4N_{e}s $, and $$ \lambda=\prod_{i=1}^{d-1}(d-i)^{0.5^{i}} $$ (7.53c)

For an arbitrary $ d $, this expression generalizes to $$ r_{e}=d\\lambda\\mu(2N\\mu)^{1-0.5^{d-1}}S^{0.5^{d-1}} $$ (7.53b) where $ S = 4N_{e}s $, and $$ \\lambda=\\prod_{i=1}^{d-1}(d-i)^{0.5^{i}} $$ (7.53c)"
}

label: Formula 7.53c
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Stochastic Tunneling
nearby_text: the sublineage containing the first two mutations. For an arbitrary $ d $, this expression generalizes to $$ r_{e}=d\lambda\mu(2N\mu)^{1-0.5^{d-1}}S^{0.5^{d-1}} $$ (7.53b) where $ S = 4N_{e}s $, and $$ \lambda=\prod_{i=1}^{d-1}(d-i)^{0.5^{i}} $$ (7.53c)

label: Formula 7.54
section: Interaction of Selection, Mutation, and Drift: Introduction
subsection: Stochastic Tunneling
nearby_text: period d - 2 additional intermediate-step mutations must be acquired, followed by the appearance of a final-step mutation destined to fixation. This leads to a rate of establishment via tunneling of $$ r_{e}\simeq4N_{e}d!(\mu/s_{d})^{d}s_{d}s_{b} $$ (7.54) which reduces to Equation 7.50 when $d = 2$. Here we see that $r_{e}$ scales with the $d$th power of the mutation rate owing to the limited opportunities for mutation during the short sojourn times o

  "nearby_text": "period d - 2 additional intermediate-step mutations must be acquired, followed by the appearance of a final-step mutation destined to fixation. This leads to a rate of establishment via tunneling of $$ r_{e}\\simeq4N_{e}d!(\\mu/s_{d})^{d}s_{d}s_{b} $$ (7.54) which reduces to Equation 7.50 when $d = 2$. Here we see that $r_{e}$ scales with the $d$th power of the mutation rate owing to the limited opportunities for mutation during the short sojourn times o"
}
